Description: Magnetite is a mixed‑valence iron oxide (Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺) that crystallizes in an inverse cubic spinel structure. In stoichiometric magnetite, Fe³⁺ occupies the tetrahedral (Tet) sites, while the octahedral (Oct) sublattice is shared equally by Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺. Magnetite occurs widely in nature as an accessory mineral in igneous, metamorphic, and sedimentary rocks, and is also produced by biological processes such as magnetotactic bacteria. Owing to its ferrimagnetic properties and electronic conductivity, magnetite is used in applications including magnetic recording, environmental remediation, catalysis, and as a functional material in geophysics and biomedical technologies.

Hematite is a ferric iron oxide (Fe³⁺₂O₃) that crystallizes in the trigonal corundum structure. In stoichiometric hematite, all iron is present as Fe³⁺ and occupies octahedral coordination with oxygen. Hematite occurs widely in nature as a major constituent of sedimentary iron formations and as an accessory mineral in igneous and metamorphic rocks, and it commonly forms through the weathering and oxidation of iron‑bearing minerals. Owing to its antiferromagnetic ordering at low temperatures, semiconducting behaviour, and chemical stability, hematite is used in applications including pigments, corrosion protection, catalysis, photoelectrochemical water splitting, and as an indicator mineral in geological and planetary studies.
